title: Strings
description: String manipulation is everywhere in interviews — from palindrome checks to parsing. Master TypeScript's string methods and template literals to handle these problems cleanly.

sections.title: String basics and access
sections.explanation: TypeScript strings are immutable sequences of characters. Access individual characters with bracket notation or `charAt()`. The `length` property gives the character count. Unlike arrays, you cannot modify a string in place — you build a new one.
sections.example:
const s: string = "interview";

console.log(s.length);    // 9
console.log(s[0]);        // i
console.log(s.charAt(3)); // e
console.log(s[s.length - 1]); // w

exercise.prompt: Given `s: string = "algorithm"`, print its length, first character (using bracket notation), fourth character (using charAt), and last character.
exercise.starterCode:
const s: string = "algorithm"

console.log(s.___)
console.log(s[___])
console.log(s.charAt(___))
console.log(s[s.length - ___])
exercise.hint: s.length for length, s[0] for first, s.charAt(3) for fourth (0-indexed), s[s.length - 1] for last.

sections.title: Template literals
sections.explanation: Template literals use backticks and `${expression}` for interpolation. They're cleaner than concatenation and essential for building output strings in interviews. They also support multiline strings without escape characters.

exercise.prompt: Given `item: string = "array"` and `count: number = 5`, use template literals to print "Found 5 array elements" and "The array has 5 items (pass)" where pass/fail depends on count > 0.
exercise.starterCode:
const item: string = "array"
const count: number = 5

console.log(`Found ${___} ${___} elements`)
console.log(`The ${item} has ${count} items (${count > 0 ? "___" : "___"})`)

validation.expected:
Found 5 array elements
The array has 5 items (pass)

validation.message: Use template literal syntax with backticks and ${}

exercise.hint: Interpolate count and item in the first template. Use a ternary inside ${} for the pass/fail check.

sections.title: Common string methods
sections.explanation: Key interview methods: `split()` to tokenize, `join()` to reassemble, `slice()` for substrings, `toLowerCase()`/`toUpperCase()` for normalization, and `includes()`/`indexOf()` for searching. These come up in nearly every string problem.
sections.example:
const s: string = "Hello World";

console.log(s.toLowerCase());      // hello world
console.log(s.split(" "));         // ["Hello", "World"]
console.log(s.slice(0, 5));        // Hello
console.log(s.includes("World")); // true
console.log(s.indexOf("o"));      // 4

const words: string[] = ["a", "b", "c"];
console.log(words.join("-"));      // a-b-c

exercise.prompt: Given `s: string = "Coding Interview Prep"`, print: the string lowercased, split into words (joined by comma), the first word using slice, whether it includes "Interview", and the index of "P".
exercise.starterCode:
const s: string = "Coding Interview Prep"

console.log(s.toLowerCase())
console.log(s.split(" ").join(","))
console.log(s.slice(0, ___))
console.log(s.includes(___))
console.log(s.indexOf(___))

validation.expected:
coding interview prep
Coding,Interview,Prep
Coding
true
16

exercise.hint: slice(0, 6) gets "Coding". includes("Interview") returns true. indexOf("P") finds the P in Prep at index 16.

sections.title: Building strings from arrays
sections.explanation: Since strings are immutable, the interview pattern is to collect characters in an array and `join()` at the end. This avoids O(n²) string concatenation. For character frequency, iterate the string and populate a Map or object.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `removeVowels` that takes a string and returns a new string with all vowels (a, e, i, o, u) removed. Collect non-vowel characters in an array and join. Print the result for "interview".

validation.message: Remove all vowels from 'interview' to get 'ntrvw'
Build result array and join at the end

exercise.hint: Check if the vowels string includes the current character. If not, push it to the result array. Join with empty string.
